questions.stem: Which of the following IS valued under AS 2?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — scope of AS 2

options.text: Cement lying at the site of a construction contract
options.explanation: Inventory held for use in construction rides with AS 7's construction WIP, outside AS 2.

options.text: A tea producer's harvested crop carried at NRV by established industry practice
options.explanation: Producers' inventories of agricultural/forest/mineral products at NRV per established practice are excluded.

options.text: Equity shares held as stock-in-trade by a dealer
options.explanation: Shares/debentures held as stock-in-trade are excluded from AS 2's scope.

options.text: Finished garments held by a manufacturer for sale in the ordinary course of business
options.explanation: Goods held for sale in the ordinary course are the core of AS 2's scope.

questions.stem: Which cost may enter the cost of inventories under AS 2?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — exclusions from cost

options.text: Storage costs of maturing whisky, where storage is a necessary stage of the production process
options.explanation: Storage is excluded UNLESS it is necessary in the production process prior to a further stage.

options.text: Salesmen's commission on sales of the finished product
options.explanation: Selling and distribution costs are always excluded from inventory cost.

options.text: Abnormal wastage of materials during a machine breakdown
options.explanation: Abnormal waste is expensed when incurred, never inventoried.

options.text: General administrative overheads of the head office
options.explanation: Admin overheads that do not bring inventory to location/condition stay out.

questions.stem: Under AS 2, where inventory items are ordinarily interchangeable, cost is determined by:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — cost formulas

options.text: FIFO or weighted average, whichever gives the fairest approximation of the cost actually incurred
options.explanation: Specific identification is for non-interchangeable/earmarked items; LIFO is not permitted at all.

options.text: Specific identification of individual costs
options.explanation: Specific identification applies only where items are NOT ordinarily interchangeable or are earmarked.

options.text: The retail method in all cases
options.explanation: The retail method is a measurement technique for suitable retail businesses, not the general formula.

options.text: LIFO, FIFO or weighted average at the enterprise's option
options.explanation: LIFO is not a permitted formula under AS 2 (Revised).

questions.topic: Ch 5 · AS 2 · Materials held for use
questions.stem: Raw material was bought at ₹100/kg; its market price has fallen to ₹85/kg. The finished product containing it is still expected to sell above its cost. The raw material is valued at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — NRV of materials held for use

options.text: ₹85 — inventory always sits at the lower of cost and market
options.explanation: The lower-of rule for materials is switched off while the finished product clears its cost.

options.text: ₹92.50 — the average of cost and replacement price
options.explanation: No averaging rule exists in AS 2.

options.text: ₹100 — materials are not written below cost while the finished product sells at or above cost
options.explanation: The material's value is realised through the product; a write-down waits until the PRODUCT falls below cost (then replacement cost may measure NRV).

options.text: Nil until the market recovers
options.explanation: Nothing supports writing a usable material off entirely.

questions.stem: Under AS 2, by-products, scrap and waste that are immaterial are measured at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — joint and by-products

options.text: Net realisable value, which is deducted from the cost of the main product
options.explanation: The main product carries joint cost minus the NRV of by-products/scrap.

options.text: Zero, with proceeds credited to other income
options.explanation: AS 2 nets the NRV against the main product's cost instead.

options.text: Full joint cost allocated on relative sales value
options.explanation: Rational allocation is for JOINT products of similar importance, not immaterial by-products.

options.text: Replacement cost
options.explanation: Replacement cost is a materials-NRV surrogate, not the by-product rule.

questions.stem: WIP has a cost of ₹90 per unit. The finished product will sell for ₹120; completion will cost ₹30 per unit and selling costs are ₹5 per unit. The WIP is carried at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — net realisable value

options.explanation: NRV = 120 − 30 − 5 = ₹85, below cost ₹90 — value at NRV.
Cost exceeds NRV (85); the lower-of rule bites.
Deducts something twice — NRV is price less completion less selling costs, once each.
Forgets the ₹30 completion cost still to be incurred.

questions.stem: Which statement about AS 10 (Revised) is correct?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — scope, bearer plants, investment property

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Bearer plants and the produce on them are both PPE
options.explanation: The produce is outside AS 10.

options.text: PPE must be used for more than three years to qualify
options.explanation: The expectation is use during more than twelve months, not three years.

options.text: Bearer plants are PPE, but the produce growing on them is not; investment property is accounted for only under the cost model
options.explanation: Both special rules sit in the standard's scope notes.

options.text: Investment property may be carried under the revaluation model
options.explanation: Investment property is restricted to the COST model.

questions.stem: A machine: list price ₹10,00,000 less 2% trade discount; refundable GST credit ₹1,20,000; freight inwards ₹40,000; installation ₹25,000; testing costs (net of sale proceeds of test output) ₹15,000; general administrative overheads ₹30,000; operating loss in the first three months ₹50,000. Its AS 10 cost is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — elements of cost

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.explanation: Drops the net testing cost, which is a directly attributable cost of readying the asset.
9,80,000 + 40,000 + 25,000 + 15,000. Refundable taxes, admin overheads and initial operating losses never enter cost.
Adds the refundable GST — only NON-refundable duties/taxes are capitalised.
Adds the initial operating loss — running below capacity after readiness is expensed.

questions.stem: PPE acquired in exchange for a non-monetary asset is measured at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — exchanges of assets

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Fair value, unless the exchange lacks commercial substance or neither fair value is reliably measurable — then the carrying amount of the asset given up
options.explanation: Fair value is the rule; carrying amount is the fallback in the two stated cases.

options.text: Nil, since no cash consideration passed
options.explanation: Non-monetary consideration still produces a measurable cost.

options.text: The lower of the two assets' fair values
options.explanation: No lower-of rule exists for exchanges.

options.text: The carrying amount of the asset given up in all cases
options.explanation: Carrying amount applies only in the exception cases.

questions.stem: Under AS 10 (Revised), spare parts, stand-by equipment and servicing equipment are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — spare parts and stand-by equipment

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Always PPE if purchased with the main machine
options.explanation: Purchase timing is irrelevant; the definition governs.

options.text: PPE when they meet the definition of PPE; otherwise inventory
options.explanation: The definition — held for use, expected use beyond twelve months — decides, not the label 'spares'.

options.text: Always inventory until actually fitted
options.explanation: Items meeting the PPE definition are PPE even before use.

options.text: Expensed on purchase
options.explanation: Neither treatment expenses them on purchase.

questions.stem: An enterprise adopting the revaluation model under AS 10 must:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — revaluation model

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Apply it asset by asset at management's choice
options.explanation: Selective revaluation within a class is exactly what the rule forbids.

options.text: Apply it to the entire class of PPE to which the asset belongs, and keep revaluations current enough that carrying amounts do not differ materially from fair value
options.explanation: Class-wide application prevents cherry-picking single appreciating assets.

options.text: Route every revaluation increase through the P&L
options.explanation: Increases go to revaluation surplus except to the extent they reverse a prior P&L decrease.

options.text: Revalue every asset annually without exception
options.explanation: The test is materiality of the gap, not a fixed annual cycle for every item.

questions.stem: An asset was earlier written DOWN by ₹50,000 through the P&L. It is now revalued UPWARD by ₹1,50,000. The credit to revaluation surplus is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — revaluation increase after a decrease

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.explanation: First ₹50,000 reverses the earlier P&L charge (credited to P&L); only the balance reaches the surplus.
Ignores that the increase first reverses the prior decrease through the P&L.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Only ₹50,000 of the increase belongs to the P&L; the remaining ₹1,00,000 is surplus.
Swaps the two components — the P&L takes 50,000, the surplus takes the rest.

questions.stem: A company switches from WDV to straight-line depreciation. Under AS 10 (Revised) this is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — review of method, life and residual value

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Prohibited unless a statute requires it
options.explanation: A change is allowed whenever the new method better reflects the consumption pattern.

options.text: A change in accounting ESTIMATE — applied prospectively over the remaining life, with AS 5 disclosure
options.explanation: The method expresses the consumption pattern, an estimate; no retrospective recomputation.

options.text: Adjusted directly against opening reserves
options.explanation: No reserves adjustment — the change works prospectively through future charges.

options.text: A change in accounting POLICY requiring retrospective recomputation
options.explanation: Retrospective recomputation is the superseded AS 6 treatment.

questions.stem: Items of PPE retired from active use and held for disposal are carried at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U2 — retirements

readLink.label: Read: AS 10 — Property, Plant and Equipment →

options.text: Nil, being no longer in use
options.explanation: Held-for-disposal assets still have realisable value.

options.text: Net realisable value in all cases
options.explanation: NRV governs only when it is BELOW carrying amount.

options.text: The lower of their carrying amount and net realisable value
options.explanation: Once retired, the asset is written down to NRV if lower, with the write-down in the P&L.

options.text: Carrying amount frozen at retirement date
options.explanation: A freeze would overstate assets whose value has fallen.

questions.stem: A current investment under AS 13 is one that is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — classification of investments

options.text: Held for less than one year, whatever its nature
options.explanation: Ready realisability is also required — intention alone is not enough.

options.text: Quoted on a recognised stock exchange
options.explanation: Quotation helps realisability but is not the definition.

options.text: Any investment made out of working capital
options.explanation: The funding source is irrelevant to classification.

options.text: By its nature readily realisable AND intended to be held for not more than one year from acquisition
options.explanation: Both limbs must hold; everything else is long-term.

questions.stem: Current investments are carried at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — carrying amount of current investments

options.text: Cost, with declines noted in the accounts
options.explanation: Cost-only carrying is the long-term rule (absent other-than-temporary decline).

options.text: The lower of cost and fair value of the portfolio as a whole
options.explanation: Overall/global valuation is expressly not considered appropriate.

options.text: Fair value, with all changes through the P&L
options.explanation: Mark-to-market through P&L is not AS 13's model.

options.text: The lower of cost and fair value, determined individually or by category — never on an overall (global) basis
options.explanation: Global netting would let gains in one scrip mask losses in another.

questions.stem: The carrying amount of long-term investments is reduced when:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — long-term investments

options.text: The portfolio of long-term investments as a whole falls below cost
options.explanation: The assessment is individual, not portfolio-wide.

options.text: There is a decline, OTHER THAN TEMPORARY, in value — judged for each investment individually, and reversed when the decline no longer exists
options.explanation: Temporary dips are ignored; the provision reverses on recovery.

options.text: Dividends are missed for two consecutive years
options.explanation: Missed dividends may be evidence, but the test is other-than-temporary decline in value.

options.text: Market value falls below cost at any balance sheet date
options.explanation: A merely temporary fall does not trigger a write-down of long-term investments.

questions.stem: An investor holds 1,000 shares bought at ₹150 each. A 1-for-5 rights issue at ₹120 is subscribed in full. The average carrying cost per share after subscription is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — rights shares subscribed

options.explanation: Ignores that the subscription cost merges into the enlarged holding.
That is the rights price alone, not the blended carrying cost.
A simple mean of the two prices ignores the 5:1 weighting.
Cost of rights (200 × 120 = 24,000) is ADDED to the holding: (1,50,000 + 24,000) ÷ 1,200 = 145.

questions.stem: Dividend received out of profits earned BEFORE the investor acquired the shares is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — dividends from pre-acquisition profits

options.text: Ignored until the investment is sold
options.explanation: The cost adjustment happens when the dividend is received.

options.text: Applied to reduce the cost of the investment, being a recovery of cost
options.explanation: Only post-acquisition earnings are income to the investor.

options.text: Credited to capital reserve
options.explanation: The credit adjusts the investment's cost, not a reserve.

options.text: Credited to the P&L like any dividend
options.explanation: Crediting pre-acquisition profit to income inflates returns bought and paid for.

questions.stem: A long-term investment (cost ₹5,00,000, carrying amount ₹4,20,000 after an other-than-temporary write-down, current fair value ₹4,10,000) is reclassified as current. It is transferred at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U3 — reclassification of investments

options.text: ₹4,20,000 — the lower of cost and carrying amount at the date of transfer
options.explanation: Long-term → current uses lower of cost and CARRYING amount; fair value drives the current→long-term direction instead.

options.text: ₹4,10,000 — fair value on the date of transfer
options.explanation: Lower-of-cost-and-FAIR-VALUE is the rule for the OPPOSITE direction (current → long-term).

options.text: ₹5,00,000 — original cost
options.explanation: Transferring at cost would silently reverse the recognised decline.

options.text: ₹4,60,000 — the mean of cost and carrying amount
options.explanation: No averaging rule exists.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a borrowing cost under AS 16?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — meaning of borrowing costs

options.text: Amortisation of discounts on debenture issues
options.explanation: Discount/premium amortisation is a listed borrowing cost.

options.text: Dividend on preference share capital not classified as a liability
options.explanation: Owners' equity — including such preference capital — is outside AS 16.

options.text: Exchange differences on foreign currency borrowings to the extent they adjust interest costs
options.explanation: Such differences are borrowing costs up to the interest a comparable rupee loan would carry.

options.text: Finance charges under an AS 19 finance lease
options.explanation: Finance-lease charges are borrowing costs.

questions.stem: Which asset QUALIFIES for borrowing-cost capitalisation?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — qualifying assets

options.text: A machine ready for its intended use when acquired
options.explanation: Assets ready on acquisition never qualify.

options.text: Whisky inventory that necessarily matures over several years before sale
options.explanation: Inventories needing a substantial period to reach saleable condition qualify.

options.text: Investments in listed equity shares
options.explanation: Financial investments are not qualifying assets.

options.text: Garments routinely manufactured in large quantities over a few weeks
options.explanation: Routinely mass-produced, quick-turn inventories are excluded.

questions.stem: Capitalisation of borrowing costs commences only when:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — commencement of capitalisation

options.text: All three hold: expenditure on the asset is being incurred, borrowing costs are being incurred, and activities necessary to prepare the asset are in progress
options.explanation: The activities limb includes technical and administrative work before physical construction.

options.text: The loan is sanctioned
options.explanation: Sanction alone incurs neither cost nor activity.

options.text: Physical construction begins
options.explanation: Pre-construction technical/administrative work already satisfies the activities limb — but the other two limbs are also needed.

options.text: The first repayment instalment falls due
options.explanation: Repayment schedules have nothing to do with commencement.

questions.stem: Construction of a bridge is halted for three months by high monsoon water levels, usual in the region. During the halt, capitalisation of borrowing costs:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — suspension of capitalisation

options.text: Is suspended, and previously capitalised amounts are reversed
options.explanation: No reversal rule exists even for genuine suspensions.

options.text: Continues — a temporary delay necessary to the process or inherent to the asset's location does not suspend capitalisation
options.explanation: Suspension is for EXTENDED interruptions of active development, not inherent seasonal pauses.

options.text: Continues only if the lender agrees
options.explanation: Lender consent is irrelevant to the accounting.

options.text: Is suspended, since active development has stopped
options.explanation: The monsoon pause is a normal incident of building bridges there — capitalisation continues.

questions.stem: A 14% loan of ₹10,00,000 was taken on 1 April specifically for a qualifying asset. Idle funds earned ₹35,000 during the year. Borrowing costs eligible for capitalisation for the year are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — specific borrowings

options.explanation: That is the deduction, not the capitalisable amount.
Income on temporarily invested specific borrowings must be deducted.
Adds the investment income instead of deducting it.
Actual costs 1,40,000 less temporary-investment income 35,000.

questions.stem: Which lease is OUTSIDE the scope of AS 19?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — scope of AS 19

options.text: A 30-year lease of land
options.explanation: Leases of land, natural-resource rights and film-type licensing agreements are all excluded.

options.text: A one-year office-equipment rental
options.explanation: Short operating leases of equipment remain within AS 19.

options.text: A 5-year lease of a delivery truck
options.explanation: Vehicle leases are squarely in scope.

options.text: A 3-year lease of factory machinery with a purchase option
options.explanation: Machinery leases are in scope; the option merely affects classification.

questions.stem: Which of the following, on its own, does NOT point to a finance lease?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — finance vs operating classification

options.text: A purchase option priced well below expected fair value
options.explanation: The bargain purchase option is a named situation.

options.text: The asset is so specialised that only the lessee can use it without major modification
options.explanation: Specialised nature is a named situation.

options.text: The lease term covers the major part of the asset's economic life
options.explanation: A named finance-lease situation.

options.text: The lessor retains responsibility for insurance and maintenance of the asset
options.explanation: Risk retention by the LESSOR points the other way — towards an operating lease.

questions.stem: At inception of a finance lease, the lessee recognises the leased asset and liability at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — lessee accounting for finance leases

options.text: The asset's cost in the lessor's books
options.explanation: The lessor's carrying amount is irrelevant to the lessee.

options.text: The fair value of the asset in all cases
options.explanation: Fair value is only one candidate; the lower figure governs.

options.text: The LOWER of the asset's fair value and the present value of minimum lease payments (discounted at the implicit rate, else the incremental borrowing rate)
options.explanation: The lower-of cap prevents capitalising more than the asset is worth.

options.text: The undiscounted total of minimum lease payments
options.explanation: Undiscounted totals ignore the financing element entirely.

questions.stem: Under a finance lease with NO reasonable certainty that the lessee will obtain ownership, the leased asset is depreciated over:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — depreciation of leased assets

options.text: The lease term in all cases
options.explanation: If the useful life is SHORTER than the term, the life governs.

options.text: Its full useful life
options.explanation: Full life applies only when ownership is reasonably certain.

options.text: Ten years by presumption
options.explanation: The ten-year presumption belongs to AS 26 intangibles, not leases.

options.text: The shorter of the lease term and its useful life
options.explanation: Without ownership certainty the lessee's use cannot outlast the lease.

questions.stem: A lessor's gross investment in a finance lease is ₹13,00,000 (minimum lease payments ₹12,00,000 + unguaranteed residual value ₹1,00,000). The present value of these components at the implicit rate is ₹9,90,000. Unearned finance income is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — lessor finance-lease income

options.explanation: Forgets the unguaranteed residual inside the gross investment (12,00,000 − 9,90,000).
That is the net investment (the receivable), not the unearned income.
UFI = gross investment − present value (net investment): 13,00,000 − 9,90,000.
That is the gross investment itself.

questions.stem: In a sale-and-leaseback where the leaseback is a FINANCE lease, the excess of sale proceeds over carrying amount is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — sale and leaseback

options.text: Credited to capital reserve permanently
options.explanation: The deferral amortises to income; it is not a permanent reserve.

options.text: Deferred and amortised over the lease term in proportion to the depreciation of the leased asset
options.explanation: The 'sale' is in substance a secured borrowing — profit cannot be front-loaded.

options.text: Netted against future rentals as they fall due
options.explanation: Amortisation follows depreciation of the asset, not the rental schedule.

options.text: Recognised in the P&L immediately
options.explanation: Immediate recognition belongs to fair-value OPERATING leasebacks.

questions.stem: Which of the following can NEVER be recognised as an asset under AS 26?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — internally generated goodwill and brands

options.text: A purchased patent
options.explanation: Purchased intangibles with measurable cost are recognisable.

options.text: Internally generated goodwill, brands, mastheads, publishing titles and customer lists
options.explanation: None is an identifiable resource controlled at reliably measurable cost.

options.text: Development expenditure meeting all six recognition criteria
options.explanation: That is precisely what AS 26 capitalises.

options.text: A licence acquired as part of an amalgamation
options.explanation: Intangibles acquired in amalgamations are recognisable at fair value under AS 14/26.

questions.stem: Where an enterprise cannot distinguish the research phase of an internal project from its development phase, the expenditure is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — research and development phases

options.text: Split 50:50 between expense and asset
options.explanation: No apportionment rule exists.

options.text: Treated as if incurred in the research phase only — expensed
options.explanation: Doubt resolves against capitalisation.

options.text: Treated as development and capitalised
options.explanation: Capitalisation needs the six demonstrations, impossible without a distinguishable phase.

options.text: Deferred until the project concludes
options.explanation: Deferral of unproven expenditure is what AS 26 forbids.

questions.stem: Which is NOT among the six conditions for capitalising development expenditure?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — recognition of development expenditure

options.text: Availability of adequate technical and financial resources to complete it
options.explanation: One of the six.

options.text: Ability to measure the attributable expenditure reliably
options.explanation: One of the six.

options.text: Technical feasibility of completing the asset
options.explanation: First of the six demonstrations.

options.text: Approval of the project by a government authority
options.explanation: The six are: technical feasibility, intention to complete, ability to use/sell, how benefits will be generated, adequate resources, reliable measurement.

questions.stem: AS 26's position on the useful life of an intangible asset is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — amortisation period

options.text: An unlimited life if cash flows are expected indefinitely
options.explanation: AS 26 requires a finite amortisation life.

options.text: A fixed maximum of ten years in all cases
options.explanation: The presumption CAN be rebutted with evidence of a longer life.

options.text: The legal term plus expected renewals in every case
options.explanation: Renewals count only when virtually certain.

options.text: A rebuttable presumption that it will not exceed ten years from availability for use; a life from legal rights cannot exceed the legal term unless renewal is virtually certain
options.explanation: Longer lives are possible with persuasive evidence — the presumption is rebuttable.

questions.stem: Development costs of ₹8,00,000 were capitalised for a process available for use on 1 April. The legal right runs 12 years, but the enterprise's best supported estimate of economic life is 8 years. Annual amortisation (straight-line, nil residual) is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — amortisation computation

options.explanation: Divides by 5 years — no such period appears in the facts.
8,00,000 over the best-estimate life of 8 years — within both the legal term and the rebuttable 10-year presumption.
Uses the 10-year presumption though a shorter best estimate (8 years) exists.
Uses the 12-year legal term, ignoring the shorter economic-life estimate.

questions.stem: The residual value of an intangible asset is assumed to be zero UNLESS:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U6 — residual value

options.text: Management estimates a resale value in good faith
options.explanation: Management optimism alone cannot displace the zero assumption.

options.text: A third party has committed to purchase it at the end of its useful life, or an active market exists that is likely to survive the asset's useful life
options.explanation: Only these two escapes rebut the zero assumption.

options.text: The asset is amortised over less than ten years
options.explanation: The amortisation period does not affect the residual assumption.

options.text: The asset was acquired rather than internally generated
options.explanation: Acquisition mode is irrelevant to residual value.

questions.stem: Which is an EXTERNAL indication of impairment under AS 28?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — indications of impairment

options.text: An internal report showing worse-than-budgeted cash flows
options.explanation: Internal evidence of underperformance.

options.text: A plan to restructure the operation the asset serves
options.explanation: Restructuring plans are internal indications.

options.text: Evidence of physical damage to the asset
options.explanation: Damage is an INTERNAL source of information.

options.text: The carrying amount of the enterprise's net assets exceeds its market capitalisation
options.explanation: A listed external signal — the market prices the whole below book.

questions.stem: Recoverable amount under AS 28 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — recoverable amount

options.text: Net selling price in all cases
options.explanation: Value in use rescues assets worth more in continued use.

options.text: The HIGHER of net selling price and value in use
options.explanation: An asset is impaired only if the better of selling it and using it falls short of carrying amount.

options.text: Value in use in all cases
options.explanation: Net selling price rescues assets worth more sold than used.

options.text: The lower of net selling price and value in use
options.explanation: The lower-of pattern belongs to inventory, not impairment.

questions.stem: An asset's carrying amount is ₹10,00,000; net selling price ₹8,20,000; value in use ₹8,80,000. The impairment loss is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — impairment loss

options.explanation: That is the gap between the two recoverable-amount candidates, not the loss.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Recoverable amount is below carrying amount, so a loss exists.
Measures against net selling price though value in use is higher.

questions.stem: In computing value in use, AS 28 requires:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — value in use

options.text: The enterprise's average historical growth applied indefinitely
options.explanation: Growth beyond budgets must be steady or DECLINING, capped at the long-term average.

options.text: Budgets covering the asset's whole remaining life
options.explanation: Formal budgets are capped at five years absent justification.

options.text: Post-tax cash flows discounted at the post-tax cost of capital
options.explanation: AS 28 works pre-tax on both flows and rate.

options.text: Cash flow projections on reasonable and supportable assumptions, budgets of at most five years, and a PRE-TAX discount rate reflecting the time value of money and asset-specific risks
options.explanation: Extrapolation beyond the budget period uses a steady or declining growth rate not above the long-term average.

questions.stem: Which statement about reversal of impairment losses is correct?
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — reversal of impairment losses

options.text: Reversals restore the asset to its recoverable amount without limit
options.explanation: The no-impairment depreciated-cost ceiling always applies.

options.text: Goodwill impairment reverses whenever the CGU recovers
options.explanation: Goodwill reversal needs the exceptional external-event condition, not mere recovery.

options.text: The increased carrying amount cannot exceed the depreciated carrying amount that would have existed had no impairment ever been recognised; goodwill reversals need a specific external event of an exceptional nature
options.explanation: Anything above the no-impairment line is revaluation (AS 10's territory), and goodwill reversal is nearly shut.

options.text: Impairment losses are never reversed
options.explanation: Reversals are required when indications show the loss has decreased — within the ceiling.

questions.topic: Ch 5 · AS 2 · Case: Narmada Fabrics (inventory valuation)
questions.case: Narmada Fabrics Ltd produced 10,000 units during the year. Costs: direct materials ₹4,00,000; direct labour ₹2,40,000; variable production overheads ₹1,60,000; fixed production overheads ₹3,00,000. Normal capacity is 12,000 units per year. Closing inventory is 1,500 units. The product's expected selling price is ₹95 per unit with selling costs of ₹3 per unit.

questions.stem: The fixed production overhead absorbed into cost PER UNIT is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — fixed overhead absorption

options.explanation: That is the labour rate hiding as overhead (2,40,000 ÷ 12,000).
An average of the two bases has no sanction.
Fixed overheads ride on NORMAL capacity: 3,00,000 ÷ 12,000 = ₹25, though only 10,000 units were made.
Divides by actual production (10,000) — low output never inflates unit cost.

questions.stem: The AS 2 cost per unit of finished goods is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — cost of conversion

options.explanation: Omits the variable production overheads.
Materials 40 + labour 24 + variable OH 16 (all on actual 10,000 units) + fixed OH 25 (on normal capacity).
Stops at variable cost, omitting fixed production overheads entirely.
Uses ₹30 fixed overhead absorbed on actual output.

questions.stem: Closing inventory is carried in the balance sheet at:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — lower of cost and NRV

options.explanation: No basis — NRV deducts the ₹3 selling cost once, giving ₹92 not ₹88.
Values at cost ₹105, ignoring that NRV is lower.
Values at the ₹95 selling price without deducting selling costs.

questions.stem: The fixed production overhead charged straight to the P&L (unabsorbed) is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U1 — unabsorbed fixed overheads

options.explanation: Uses a ₹30 rate for the shortfall — the rate stays at ₹25.
3,00,000 incurred − 25 × 10,000 absorbed = ₹50,000 expensed as period cost.
Halves the shortfall without basis; 2,000 units × 25 = 50,000.

options.text: Nil
options.explanation: Absorption at normal capacity leaves 2,000 units' worth unabsorbed.

questions.topic: Ch 5 · AS 16 · Case: Chambal Power (borrowing costs)
questions.case: Chambal Power Ltd is building a plant (a qualifying asset) through the year ended 31 March. A specific 12% loan of ₹40,00,000 was drawn on 1 April; pending use, idle funds earned interest of ₹60,000. The company's general borrowings throughout the year were ₹25,00,000 at 10% and ₹25,00,000 at 14%. Average expenditure on the plant funded from general borrowings was ₹10,00,000.

questions.stem: Borrowing costs capitalisable from the SPECIFIC loan are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — specific borrowings

options.explanation: Adds the investment income instead of deducting it.
That is the deduction, not the capitalisable cost.
12% × 40,00,000 = 4,80,000 less temporary-investment income 60,000.
Ignores the ₹60,000 earned on temporarily invested funds.

questions.stem: The capitalisation rate for general borrowings is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — capitalisation rate

options.explanation: The cheaper loan's rate alone — the pool is blended.
The dearer loan's rate alone.
Mis-weights the equal pools; equal amounts blend to the simple mean, 12%.

questions.stem: Total borrowing costs capitalised for the year are:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U4 — total capitalisation

options.explanation: Leaves out the general-borrowings component.
Leaves out the specific-loan component.
Uses the gross specific cost (4,80,000) without deducting investment income.

questions.topic: Ch 5 · AS 19 · Case: Tapti Logistics (lessee finance lease)
questions.case: Tapti Logistics Ltd leases a fleet vehicle system for 5 years at ₹2,50,000 per year payable at each year end. The fair value of the asset at inception is ₹10,00,000. The interest rate implicit in the lease is 10% (5-year annuity PV factor 3.791). There is no reasonable certainty that ownership will pass.

questions.stem: The lease is classified as:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — classification

options.text: A finance lease only if the lessor agrees in writing
options.explanation: Classification follows substance at inception, not consent.

options.text: A finance lease — the present value of minimum lease payments (≈95% of fair value) is substantially all of the asset's fair value
An operating lease — payments are annual, not monthly
options.explanation: Payment frequency is irrelevant to classification.

options.text: An operating lease — title does not pass
options.explanation: Title transfer is not required; risks-and-rewards substance governs.

questions.stem: The asset and liability recognised at inception amount to:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — initial measurement

options.explanation: The undiscounted rentals ignore the financing element.
No computation in the facts produces this figure — discount the rentals at 10%.
Fair value loses to the lower PV of minimum lease payments.
PV of MLP (2,50,000 × 3.791), which is LOWER than the ₹10,00,000 fair value.

questions.stem: The finance charge for Year 1 is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — finance charge allocation

options.explanation: 10% of the undiscounted rental total has no meaning.
10% on fair value — the rate applies to the recognised liability.
10% on the opening liability of 9,47,750.
That is the whole rental; only its interest component is a finance charge.

questions.stem: The lease liability outstanding at the END of Year 1 (after the payment) is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U5 — liability run-off

options.explanation: Accrues interest but forgets the year-end payment.
Deducts the full rental without first accruing the year's interest.
Reduces the liability by 10% — the mechanics are interest-accrual minus payment.

questions.topic: Ch 5 · AS 28 · Case: Sabarmati Chemicals (CGU impairment)
questions.case: A cash-generating unit of Sabarmati Chemicals Ltd comprises: allocated goodwill ₹1,00,000; plant and machinery ₹6,00,000; intangible assets ₹2,00,000 — total carrying amount ₹9,00,000. Following an adverse regulatory change, the unit's recoverable amount is determined at ₹6,75,000. No individual asset's net selling price or value in use is separately determinable.

questions.stem: The impairment loss for the CGU is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — CGU impairment loss

options.explanation: Carrying 9,00,000 − recoverable 6,75,000.
No computation in the facts gives this — subtract recoverable from carrying.
Overshoots; the gap is exactly 2,25,000.
That is the loss remaining AFTER goodwill absorbs its share.

questions.stem: The loss allocated to PLANT AND MACHINERY is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — allocation order

options.explanation: Pro-rates the full 2,25,000 over plant and intangibles, skipping the goodwill-first step.
Pro-rates the whole loss over all assets INCLUDING goodwill — goodwill goes first.
Goodwill absorbs 1,00,000 first; the remaining 1,25,000 splits pro-rata 6:2 → plant 93,750.
That is the residual loss after goodwill, before pro-rating between the two assets.

questions.stem: The carrying amount of plant and machinery after the impairment is:
questions.sourceRef: SM Ch 5 U7 — post-impairment carrying amounts

options.explanation: Deducts 1,68,750 — the skip-goodwill error.
Plant must absorb its pro-rata share once goodwill is exhausted.
Deducts 1,50,000 — the all-assets pro-rata error.

questions.stem: What is included in, and excluded from, the cost of inventories under AS 2? (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Purchase cost: price + non-recoverable duties/taxes + freight inward and other directly attributable acquisition costs − trade discounts and rebates

Conversion cost: direct labour; variable production overheads on actual output; fixed production overheads absorbed at NORMAL capacity (shortfall expensed; abnormally high output cuts the rate to actual)
Other costs only so far as incurred to bring inventory to present location and condition
Excluded: abnormal waste; storage (unless a necessary production stage); administrative overheads; selling and distribution costs

questions.stem: State the elements of cost of an item of PPE under AS 10 (Revised), giving two examples of costs that are NOT part of cost. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Purchase price including import duties and non-refundable taxes, net of trade discounts and rebates
Directly attributable costs of bringing the asset to location and condition: site preparation, delivery/handling, installation/assembly, professional fees, net testing costs
Initial estimate of decommissioning, restoration and similar obligations
Not cost (any two): new-facility opening costs, advertising, administration/general overheads, initial operating losses, relocation costs

questions.stem: Explain the treatment of increases and decreases on revaluation of PPE and the disposal of the revaluation surplus. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Model applies to an entire CLASS; revaluations kept sufficiently current
Increase → revaluation surplus; but credited to P&L to the extent it reverses a previous P&L decrease of the same asset
Decrease → P&L; but debited to surplus to the extent a credit balance exists for that asset
Surplus transferred to revenue reserves on derecognition, or progressively by the depreciation differential — never through the P&L

questions.stem: State the carrying-amount rules for current and long-term investments and the rules on reclassification between the two. (4 marks)

skeleton.point: Current: lower of cost and fair value, individually or by category — never on an overall/global basis
Long-term: cost, reduced individually for declines other than temporary; reduction reversed when it no longer exists
Long-term → current: transfer at lower of cost and carrying amount at the transfer date
Current → long-term: transfer at lower of cost and fair value at the transfer date

questions.stem: When does capitalisation of borrowing costs commence, get suspended and cease under AS 16? (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Commencement — all three: expenditure being incurred; borrowing costs being incurred; activities necessary to prepare the asset in progress (including technical/administrative work)
Suspended while active development stands interrupted for an EXTENDED period
No suspension for temporary delays necessary to the process or inherent to the asset/locality (e.g. usual monsoon interruption)
Cessation when substantially all preparatory activities are complete; part-wise cessation for parts usable independently

questions.stem: State the situations which would normally lead a lease to be classified as a finance lease under AS 19. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Ownership passes to the lessee once the term ends
Bargain purchase option expected to be exercised
Lease term covers the major part of the asset's economic life even without title transfer
PV of minimum lease payments at inception ≥ substantially all of the asset's fair value
Asset of specialised nature usable only by the lessee without major modifications (supporting indicators: lessee bears cancellation losses / residual fluctuations; bargain renewal)

questions.stem: State the conditions an enterprise must demonstrate to capitalise development expenditure under AS 26. (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Technical feasibility of completing the intangible asset for use or sale
Intention to complete, and ability to use or sell, the asset
How the asset will generate probable future economic benefits (market for output/asset, or internal usefulness)
Adequate technical, financial and other resources to complete and use/sell
Reliable measurement of attributable expenditure; research (or indistinguishable projects) always expensed

questions.stem: How is an impairment loss of a cash-generating unit allocated, and what limits apply to reversals of impairment losses? (5 marks)

skeleton.point: Loss allocated FIRST to goodwill allocated to the CGU
Balance pro-rata to other assets on their carrying amounts; no asset below the highest of its NSP, VIU and zero (excess re-allocated)
Reversal ceiling: carrying amount may not exceed depreciated carrying amount had no impairment been recognised; excess would be revaluation (AS 10)
Goodwill reversal only where the loss arose from a specific external event of exceptional nature not expected to recur, reversed by subsequent external events
